coredata.dc:title: Doping carbon dots on etched CeO2-based bimetallic hydroxides for improved photocatalytic ammonia production
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: International Solar Energy Society. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Huaiwei
Bao, Liang
Pan, Ying
Ge, Jing-yuan
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic ammonia synthesis with environmentally friendly process has received increasing attentions. Herein, carbon dots doped CeO2/Co-Zn bimetallic hydroxides with all-solid-state structure are assembled firstly, and an overall photocatalytic nitrogen fixation process is achieved. These types of photocatalysts show more excellent performances and higher reaction activities. The NH3 production rates can achieve about 548 and 985 µmol·gcal −1·h−1 under the visible-light and full-spectrum illumination, which is one of the best reported performances for the N2 photo-fixation. The improved photocatalytic performances are attributed to the enhancement of N2 adsorption active sites and photo-generated charge-hole separation efficiencies. This work provides a novel insight fabrication strategy to further develop the suitable ammonia synthesis photocatalysts under a mild condition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-noble
Etching
Photocatalytic
Ammonia

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the various catalysts.
TEM-EDS mapping (a) and HRTEM (b) images of the CeO2-CH/CDs sample.
XPS spectra of catalysts (a) Zn 2p; (b) Co 2p; (c) Ce 3d; (d) O 1 s.
Raman (a) and EPR spectra (b) of the catalysts.

(a) NH3 produced yields of the samples (inset figure is the cycle stability for the CeO2-CH/CDs sample); (b) NH3 production rates photocatalyzed by 1 g of CeO2-CH/CDs sample; (c) NH3 photogenerated amounts of CeO2-CH/CDs sample; (d) Calculated AQEs for the CeO2-CH/CDs sample.
PL emission spectra (a), photocurrent responses (b), EIS spectra (c) and N2-TPD profiles (d) of the various samples.
(a) Band gap energy (inset figure reveals the M−S curves); (b) EPR signal for DMPO-·O2 – and DMPO-·OH under light irradiation; (c,d) Possible mechanism of photocatalytic N2 fixation process.
(a) FTIR analyses during N2 photofixation process of CeO2-CH/CDs sample; (b) Possible pathway during the photoreduction process.

head.ce:title: Doping carbon dots on etched CeO2-based bimetallic hydroxides for improved photocatalytic ammonia production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic ammonia synthesis with environmentally friendly process has received increasing attentions. Herein, carbon dots doped CeO2/Co-Zn bimetallic hydroxides with all-solid-state structure are assembled firstly, and an overall photocatalytic nitrogen fixation process is achieved. These types of photocatalysts show more excellent performances and higher reaction activities. The NH3 production rates can achieve about 548 and 985 µmol·gcal −1·h−1 under the visible-light and full-spectrum illumination, which is one of the best reported performances for the N2 photo-fixation. The improved photocatalytic performances are attributed to the enhancement of N2 adsorption active sites and photo-generated charge-hole separation efficiencies. This work provides a novel insight fabrication strategy to further develop the suitable ammonia synthesis photocatalysts under a mild condition.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia is an important chemical product which is widely used in many fields. The traditional Haber-Bosch method consumes a large amount of energy [1–4]. In contrast, photocatalytic nitrogen fixation is an environmentally friendly process with low consumptions. During N2 photo-fixation process, how to efficiently break the N≡N bonds with the high binding energy (940 KJ·mol−1) is the grandest challenge [5–7]. This implies that the photocatalysts should have more active sites and stronger nitrogen adsorption ability to break the triple bond [6,8–11].

Cerium dioxide, as a n-type semiconductor, has received more attentions in the photocatalytic field for the rich surface defects and low cost [12–14]. CeO2 has the reversible charge Ce3+/Ce4+pairs, which exhibit the excellent redox abilities [15,16]. But fast electron-hole recombination and less visible light absorption have seriously limited its further application. Therefore, a lot of works have been done to further improve the CeO2-based photocatalysts properties. Jia et al reported an assembly strategy of crystalline ceria and gold nanorods to obtain near-infrared photocatalytic N2 fixation [17]. Pu et al. synthesized Cu2O-doped CeO2 catalysts, which efficiently enhanced the charge-hole pairs separation ability [18]. Feng et al developed a heterojunction photocatalyst of CeCO3OH/g-C3N4/CeO2, and the special structures provided a higher charge carrier separation efficiency. The catalyst exhibited improved nitrogen photo-fixation activity [19]. Parida et.al prepared a phosphide protected FeS2-FeP-CeO2 NS ternary nanohybrid, and the remarked N2 fixation performances were obtained [20]. CeO2-BiFeO3 p-n heterojunction photocatalyst was also reported. The designed oxygen vacancies and the double charges migration process caused a remarkable N2 photo-fixation performance [21]. Some other ceria-based catalysts [22–29] were also widely used in photocatalytic process.

However, the visible-light heterojunction photocatalysts still face with some bottlenecks, including the unsatisfying charge absorption/transfer efficiency and various lower band gaps for semiconductors. All-solid-state photocatalytic systems have been widely used in the photocatalysis for the higher photo-induced charge-hole separation efficiencies [30,31]. The noble-metal as the usual mediator hindered their further widespread applications, and carbon dots (CDs) are considered the suitable candidate for their lower cost and excellent photo-generated electron transfer rate [32–35]. S-scheme heterostructures with CDs mediator of g-C3N4/CDs/BiOBr [36], CdS/CDs/BiOCl [37], BiVO4/CDs/CdS [38], CeCO3OH/CDs/Co(OH)2 [39] and CeO2-MOH/CDs [40] are reported, and the improved photocatalytic properties were also achieved.

In the study, a simple assembly strategy of etched CeO2/Co-Zn bimetallic hydroxides photocatalyst with carbon dots charges mediator is concerned, in which CeO2 and CH compositions can act as the reduction site and oxidation site, respectively. The sample exhibits outstanding N2 reduction performances. CDs-doped S-scheme heterojunctions offer more photoreaction active sites to enhance surface adsorption performances and photo-generated charge-hole separations.

Firstly, the CeO2-CZH precursor sample is prepared by traditional hydrothermal process. 1.89 g Zn(NO3)2, 2.92 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 3.26 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O are injected into 20 ml distilled water, and 2.4 g NaOH added in another 20 ml deionized water. Then the two solutions are mixed at 333 K. The products are separated through centrifugation, and washed several times with deionized water and alcohol. Finally, the various catalysts are dried for 12 h at 333 K and signed as CeO2-CZH. The CeO2-CH and CeO2-CH/CDs catalysts are synthesized via the nexthydrothermal etching stage. Typically, 0.4 g CeO2-CZH sample is put into 90 ml NaOH solution (1 mol/L), and thendispersed for 1 h. The mixed solutions put into a 120 ml sealed reactor and heated at 393 K for 1 h. The separated residues are dried, which named as CeO2-CH. Then, 0.2 g CeO2-CH and 0.17 g L-ascorbic acid dissolved in 30 ml deionized water in a 40 ml sealed hydrothermal reactor and heat-treated at 433 K for 24 h. The obtained catalyst is CeO2-CH/CDs. Similar synthesis processes of the CeO2-CZH/CDs, CeO2-CH/CDs-1 and CeO2-CH/CDs-2 are operated except the various etching times of 0 h, 2 h and 3 h, respectively. The nitrogen contents in the as-prepared samples are measured accurately, and there are hardly detected in the various catalysts. These results ensure that the N atoms of the formed NH3 are originated from foreign N2.

Nitrogen photo-fixation measurements are performed using a 300 W Xenon light source. Typically, 100 mg as-prepared catalyst and 250 ml deionized water are put into a home-made quartz photocatalytic reactor with a circulating water system. The aqueous suspension is dispersed in ultrasonic wave for 30 min in dark, and bubbled with pure N2 at the same time. The catalytic reaction is processed under the light illumination for 1 h. Ammonia production yields are analyzed via ion chromatography and Nessler's reagent method. The standard curves are shown in Fig. S1. Specific details are illustrated in Supplementary Materials.

Phase structures of the photocatalysts are measured via XRD in Fig. 1 . It obviously illustrates that the diffraction peaks correspond to cubic CeO2 (JCPDS #34-0394) and hexagonal Co(OH)2 (JCPDS #30-0443) for pure CeO2 and CH samples, respectively, and the two different peaks are all detected in CeO2-CH sample. There are no obvious Zn-based phases in the CH composites due to the substitution of Co ions for Zn which have the very similar ions radius. Comparing to the original CeO2-CZH, the peaks of Zn-Co-O composites (Zn2Co3(OH)10·2H2O) are disappearing and a uniform CH phase is formed in the CeO2-CH sample. Fig. S2 shows a schematic of the morphological evolution of the photo-catalyst for guiding our discussion of the N2 fixation data below. The original sample (CeO2-CZH) contains large amounts of Zn-based composition, and this composition will be etched partly by alkali solution. A facile etching process can produce some homogeneous phases and high unsaturated Zn sites exposition [41,42]. The phase compositions of the various carbon dots doped samples are shown in Fig. S3. There are no obvious characteristic peaks for carbon in the patterns of the carbon doped samples due to the relatively small number of CDs in the samples.

TEM images of CeO2-CZH/CDs and CeO2-CH/CDs samples are shown in Fig. S4. From the pictures, nanosheets and nanoparticles structures are all existed in the CeO2-CZH/CDs sample, and most of the nanosheets are transformed to the nanoparticles during the etching process in the CeO2-CH/CDs. TEM-EDS analysis is also applied to distinguish the different element. As shown in Fig. 2 a, all the elements including Ce, Zn, Co, O and C are detected uniformly. HRTEM pictures of CeO2-CH/CDs are illustrated in Fig. 2b. The image displayed that three nanostructures of CeO2, CH and CDs with lattice constants of 0.312 nm ((111) plane of CeO2), 0.276 nm ((100) plane of Co(OH)2) and 0.21 nm ((100) plane of graphite) are coexisting in the sample [36,39,40,43]. More relevantly, the obvious interfacial transition zones among these nanoparticles can be found, which means the generation of CDs doped CeO2/CH heterojunction.

Surface valance states are analyzed through the XPS measurement in Fig. S5 and Fig. 3 . Zn 2p XPS spectra for the photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 3a. Two spin–orbit multiples of Zn 2p3/2 (Zn1 and Zn2) and Zn 2p1/2 (Zn1ʹ and Zn2ʹ) are detected in the picture. The peaks with the binding energies of about 1021.5 eV (Zn2) and 1045.2 (Zn2ʹ) are corresponding to the Zn element in the wurtzite phase. Other two peaks with the binding energies of around 1024.7 eV (Zn1) and 1048.3(Zn1ʹ) are corresponding to the ZnO-OH group [44,45]. Co 2p XPS spectra are also shown in Fig. 3b. Two main peaks all get deviated to highenergy direction for the CDs doped sample. The peak at about 779.8 eV in CeO2-CH is assigned to the Co3+ ions in Co3O4 phase, and the peak at 781.1 eV in CeO2-CH/CDs attributed to the Co2+ in Co(OH)2 [46,47]. Four pairs can be detected in the Ce 3d XPS spectra (Fig. 3c), and the peaks signed as µ (µ, µ1, µ2 and µ3) and ν (ν, ν1, ν2, and ν3) attributed to the Ce 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 stations, respectively. The ν1(884.1 eV) and µ1 (902.1 eV) are attributed to the Ce3+, and ν (881.7 eV) and µ (900.5 eV), ν2 (888.6 eV) and µ2 (907.9 eV), ν3 (897.9 eV) and µ3 (916.6 eV), are the Ce4+ [48–50]. The binding energies for the CeO2-CZH and CeO2-CH are almost no differences. In the CeO2-CH/CDs system, almost all the Ce4+ are reduced into the Ce3+, and much more Ce4+/Ce3+ redox are formed.

For C1s XPS curves (Fig. S6), the peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.5 eV, 287.1 eV and 288.8 eV are corresponding to the C = C, C-C, C-O-C and C = O bonds [51,52]. The increase of C = O peak area and the presence of new C-O-C peaks indicate the CDs formation in the CeO2-CH/CDs composite [43,53]. In Fig. 3d, the peaks with the binding energy of 529.6 eV, 531.6 eV and 532.8 eV are corresponding to lattice oxygen (Ol), surface oxygen vacancies (Ov) and carbonyl groups or hydroxide (Oa) [54,55]. After the etching process, some new edge planes are exposed in the water-oxygen circumstance. The oxygen atom fills into the defects, thus decreasing the Ov concentration. More and more oxygen vacancies can be regenerated on the exposed sites after carbon doped process.

The catalysts are also characterized via the solid-state Raman spectrum in Fig. 4 a. A single peak with a high intensity at about 465 cm−1 is corresponding to Ce-O F2g stretching mode [56,57]. Raman spectrum of CeO2-CZH displays a relatively strong band at about 700 cm−1, which can confirm the existence of Zn-Co-O compounds in this sample [58,59]. This strong peak will be disappeared after the etching process, and the homogeneous phases came into being gradually in this period. The peak at about 600 cm−1 is attributed to the Ov and other surface defects caused by the coordination effects of the various ions [60,61]. The peak of the etched sample is weakened slightly due to the decrease of surface oxygen vacancy, and this corresponds to the XPS surface analyses. Two broad peaks around about 1370 and 1590 cm−1 are corresponding to the D and G carbon-based bands, respectively. CDs are well dispersed in the CeO2-CH/CDs catalyst [53,62]. The states of surface oxygen defect (Ov) states are analyzed via the EPR measurement in Fig. 4b and Fig. S7. The paramagnetic signal with the g value of 2.003 can be found in the samples. Interestingly, the excess photo-generated defects are found under the light illumination conditions, which can enhance the nitrogen fixation properties. This result has also been proved in some previous studies [41,63]. The signal of light-induced Ov in CeO2-CH/CDs is much higher than the others. More in-situ Ov will be formed on the sample surface during the photocatalytic process.

The nitrogen photo-fixation rates for the various catalysts are displayed in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S8. CeO2-CZH and CeO2-CH have lower activities under either visible or full-spectrum light irradiation. Fig. S8 shows the NH3 production rates of the CDs doped samples. All the doped catalysts have good photocatalytic N2 reduction activities, and the etching samples reveal better performances. The ammonia production rates of CeO2-CH/CDs will reach 985 and 548 µmol·gcal −1·h−1 under full-spectrum and visible light illumination, and the photocatalyst also displays an excellent stability. On the other hand, NH4 + and NO3 – are also confirmed through ion chromatograph (IC) method (Fig. S9). The carbon bridging sample also exhibits a competitive performance compared with some recent reports (Table S1). Otherwise, there is no by-product hydrazine in the residual solutions. Overall N2 photo-fixation process to produce nitrate and ammonia are occurred in the photoreactions. The similar results were also obtained in some other reports [64,65].

The introduction of CDs as the charge transport mediator in photocatalysts can significantly improve the N2 photo-fixation performances. Moreover, the produced NH3 rates catalyzed by 1 g catalyst (CeO2-CH/CDs) in 1L solution under alternative light irradiation at intervals of 0.5 h are displayed in Fig. 5b. No NH3 is generated without the light, and the photocatalytic reactions are driven through the light illuminations. Total NH3 generated capacity of CeO2-CH/CDs with N2 or Ar flow under the light irradiation are detected. The production rate achieved above 1600 μmol (1 g sample) within two hours under N2 gas flow, and no NH3 generated in Ar gas. The result further confirms that the N atom of the formed NH3 is originated from foreign N2 gas. The AQEs of CeO2-CH/CDs are measured in Fig. 5d, and the AQE data at 420 nm is above 0.55%.

The optical absorption performances of various catalysts are identified via diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectrum. From Fig. S10, CeO2-CH/CDs sample shows the higher absorption ability in the visible range. The band gap is calculated through the K-M function in Fig. 7 a. The band gaps with 2.92 eV and 1.96 eV are indicated for the CeO2 and CH phase, respectively. Otherwise, band positions are evaluated via the M−S curves in the Fig. 7a. CeO2 is n-semiconductor and CH is p-semiconductor. The M−S curve of CeO2-CH/CDs reveals an inverted “V-shape” with both negative and positive slopes, which further prove the generation of p-n type heterojunction [69–72]. Otherwise, the Vfb (flat-band) values of CH and CeO2 are 1.31 V (vs. NHE) and 0.07 V (vs. NHE). Under normal conditions, the conduction band value (Vcb) is more negative than the Vfb by 0.1 V for n-type semiconductors, and the valence band (Vvb) is more positive than the Vfb by 0.1 V for p-type [73–75]. The CeO2 Vcb (-0.03 V) and CH Vvb (1.41 V) can be detected according to the Vfb values. Free radical trapping measurements are detected via EPR method in Fig. 7(b). No obvious O2 – signals are found for the based CeO2 sample. The sample exhibits high signals for both DMPOOH and DMPOO2 –, showing that the as-prepared structures simultaneously produce a large number of OH andO2 – radicals with the light excitation. The groups are recognized as the important active species in the photocatalytic reactions. More specifically, photo-generated charges or holes have appropriate redox potentials to generate OH or O2 – radicals.

In the CeO2-CH/CDs sample, the several compositions not possibly have done the N2 fixation process alone under light irradiation (Fig. S11a). The reduction level of CeO2 is lower than N2/NH3 redox potential, and the oxidation level of CH is higher than OH–/OH potential. When CH and CeO2 are connected, the p-n heterojunction will be generated in Fig. S11b. Their different Fermi level can induce an internal electric field on the interface, which can improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons/holes pairs [32,76–78]. Let us suppose that type II p-n heterostructure is generated in the photocatalytic system, as shown as Fig. 7c. Under light illumination, photoinduced charges on the CB of CH migrate to CeO2, and photoinduced holes on the VB of CeO2 will transfer to CH at the same time. However, the CeO2 reduction level is higher than N2/NH3 potential, and there will be no NH3 generation. So, the enhanced photocatalytic activities in CeO2-CH/CDs can be illustrated by S-scheme heterojunctions in Fig. 7d and Fig. S11c, of which CDs can act as the electron migration mediator [36,37,79]. The heterojunction compositions of CeO2 and CH are stimulated under light irradiation, and then the VB holes in the CH phase and the CB electrons in the CeO2 phase can be excited. The photo-generated charges and holes move to the surface of CDs transfer mediator, and redistributeon CeO2 VB and CH CB, respectively. Finally, the good reduction ability of CH phase (-0.55 V) and oxidation ability of CeO2 phase (2.89 V) can provide enough energy to complete photocatalytic N2 fixation process.

The FT-IR spectra of the CeO2-CH/CDs sample with the reaction duration are illustrated in Fig. 8 a. The O-H or N-H stretching mode are mainly in the 3000–3700 cm−1 [39,41], and the signal intensity slightly increase with the light irritation time. The wavenumber at 1120 and 1660 cm−1 are belong to the N-N stretching and NH4 + physical absorption [80]. Otherwise, two weak peaks anchored at 1330 and 1480 cm−1 are usually consider as the –NH2 wagging mode and H-N-H bending mode, respectively [81,82]. Herein, the ammonia production process in this system could be summarized the associative distal pathway because of the generation of *NH and *NH2 but no *N2H4.The process is shown in Fig. 8b. At first, the adsorbed *N2 bond can combine one photo-induced electron (e−) and one proton (H+) to generate the *N2H. Then another H+/e− pairs will get attached to the end of *N2H to form one NH3 molecule. The next molecule can be formed through combining the other three H+/e− pairs on the leaving *N bond.

In this work, etched CeO2-based bimetallic hydroxides photocatalysts of CeO2-CDs-CH composites with the carbon dots electrons mediator were reported for the improved photocatalytic N2 fixation performances. The samples exhibited the remarkable performances due to more photo-excitation oxygen vacancies and remarkable nitrogen adsorption abilities. The NH3 produced rate can reach approximately 985 µmol·gcal −1·h−1 under light illumination without any sacrificial agents. The as-prepared photocatalyst also exhibits stronger EPR signals in the DMPOOH andO2 – trapping process, the heterojunction structure can simultaneously produce a large number of OH andO2 – radicals under the light illumination. The AQEs at 420 nm has achieved to above 0.55 %. Our work can provide a promising guidance for the application of CeO2-based S-scheme photocatalysts in large-scale photocatalytic N2 fixation process.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research funded by the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY20B010003)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.solener.2023.112002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in electrochemical synthesis of ammonia from nitrogen: strategies to improve the catalytic activity and selectivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yang, W. Ding, H. Zhang, S. Zhang, Recent progress in electrochemical synthesis of ammonia from nitrogen: strategies to improve the catalytic activity and selectivity, Energy & Environmental Science, 14 (2021) 672-687.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. Qureshi, M. Mumtaz, F.K. Chong, A. Mukhtar, S. Saqib, S. Ullah, M. Mubashir, K.S. Khoo, P.L. Show, A review on sensing and catalytic activity of nano-catalyst for synthesis of one-step ammonia and urea: Challenges and perspectives, Chemosphere, 291 (2022) 132806-132806.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in transition metal sulfide-based electrocatalysts and photocatalysts for nitrogen fixation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.H. Dolla, T. Matthews, N.W. Maxakato, P. Ndungu, T. Montini, Recent advances in transition metal sulfide-based electrocatalysts and photocatalysts for nitrogen fixation, Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry, 928 (2023).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photochemical Ammonia Synthesis over Reduced Polyoxovanadate-Intercalated Defective ZnAl Layered Double Hydroxide Nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.R. Li, T. Ji, W.C. Chen, W. Du, Y.H. Wang, Y.A. Hou, W.L. Chen, Photochemical Ammonia Synthesis over Reduced Polyoxovanadate-Intercalated Defective ZnAl Layered Double Hydroxide Nanosheets, Inorganic Chemistry, (2023).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Greening Ammonia toward the Solar Ammonia Refinery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, M. Xia, H. Wang, K. Huang, C. Qian, C.T. Maravelias, G.A. Ozin, Greening Ammonia toward the Solar Ammonia Refinery, Joule, 2 (2018) 1055-1074.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fundamentals and Recent Progress of Photocatalytic Nitrogen-Fixation Reaction over Semiconductors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chen, D. Liu, T. Peng, Fundamentals and Recent Progress of Photocatalytic Nitrogen-Fixation Reaction over Semiconductors, Solar Rrl, 5 (2021) 2000487.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Managing the Nitrogen Cycle via Plasmonic (Photo)Electrocatalysis: Toward Circular Economy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nazemi, M.A. El-Sayed, Managing the Nitrogen Cycle via Plasmonic (Photo)Electrocatalysis: Toward Circular Economy, Accounts of Chemical Research, 54 (2021) 4294-4304.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in photocatalytic nitrogen fixation and beyond

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wei, W. Jiang, Y. Liu, X. Bai, D. Hao, B.-J. Ni, Recent advances in photocatalytic nitrogen fixation and beyond, Nanoscale, 14 (2022) 2990-2997.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural design and control of photocatalytic nitrogen-fixing catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Xia, Y.Y. Xu, X.Y. Yu, K. Chang, H. Gong, X.L. Fan, X.G. Meng, X.L. Huang, T. Wang, J.P. He, Structural design and control of photocatalytic nitrogen-fixing catalysts, Journal of Materials Chemistry A, 10 (2022) 17377-17394.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent Advances in Application of Graphitic Carbon Nitride-Based Catalysts for Photocatalytic Nitrogen Fixation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhang, S.Q. Hou, T.Y. Wang, S.X. Liu, X.C. Gao, C.Y. Wang, G.X. Wang, Recent Advances in Application of Graphitic Carbon Nitride-Based Catalysts for Photocatalytic Nitrogen Fixation, Small, 18 (2022).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Steric hindrance-induced selective growth of rhodium on gold nanobipyramids for plasmon-enhanced nitrogen fixation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.L. Jia, F. Li, Y.Y. Yang, M.X. Zhao, J.Z. Li, C.Y. Zhang, Steric hindrance-induced selective growth of rhodium on gold nanobipyramids for plasmon-enhanced nitrogen fixation, Chemical Science, 14 (2023) 5656-5664.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fundamentals and catalytic applications of CeO2-based materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Montini, M. Melchionna, M. Monai, P. Fornasiero, Fundamentals and catalytic applications of CeO2-based materials, Chemical Reviews, 116 (2016) 5987-6041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Syntheses and applications of noble-metal-free CeO2-based mixed-oxide nanocatalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.T. Yang, X. Wang, S.Y. Song, H.J. Zhang, Syntheses and applications of noble-metal-free CeO2-based mixed-oxide nanocatalysts, Chem, 5 (2019) 1743-1774.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ceria and ceria-based nanostructured materials for photoenergy applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.L. Xie, Z.L. Wang, F.L. Cheng, P. Zhang, W.J. Mai, Y.X. Tong, Ceria and ceria-based nanostructured materials for photoenergy applications, Nano Energy, 34 (2017) 313-337.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ceria-Based Materials for Thermocatalytic and Photocatalytic Organic Synthesis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.B. Huang, K.Y. Zhang, B.X. Peng, G. Wang, M. Muhler, F. Wang, Ceria-Based Materials for Thermocatalytic and Photocatalytic Organic Synthesis, Acs Catalysis, 11 (2021) 9618-9678.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, L. Cao, F.S. Lu, M.J. Meziani, H. Li, G. Qi, B. Zhou, B.A. Harruff, F. Kermarrec, Y.P. Sun, Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots, Chemical Communications, (2009) 3774-3776.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient visible light nitrogen fixation with BiOBr nanosheets of oxygen vacancies on the exposed 001 facets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, J. Shang, Z. Ai, L. Zhang, Efficient visible light nitrogen fixation with BiOBr nanosheets of oxygen vacancies on the exposed {001} facets, J. Am.Chem. Soc., 137 (2015) 6393-6399.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical nitrogen reduction to ammonia at ambient conditions on nitrogen and phosphorus Co-doped porous carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Song, H. Wang, L. Kang, B. Ran, H. Song, R. Wang, Electrochemical nitrogen reduction to ammonia at ambient conditions on nitrogen and phosphorus Co-doped porous carbon, Chem. Commun., 55 (2019) 687-690.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Spectroscopic Study on the Nitrogen Electrochemical Reduction Reaction on Gold and Platinum Surfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yao, S. Zhu, H. Wang, H. Li, M. Shao, A Spectroscopic Study on the Nitrogen Electrochemical Reduction Reaction on Gold and Platinum Surfaces, J.Am.Chem. Soc., 140 (2018) 1496-1501.
